items.name: Cutting Chai (tapri)
items.fact: A cutting chai cost just ₹1 in 1990 — it's now ₹15 at most tapris, a 15x rise in 35 years that still makes it India's greatest bargain.

items.name: Matchbox (50 sticks)
items.fact: The matchbox held its price at ₹1 for nearly two decades — it's one of India's most stubborn inflation resistors, controlled partly by government price sensitivity.

items.name: Samosa (single, street)
items.fact: A street samosa cost ₹1 in 1990 — today it's ₹20, a 20x inflation that outruns even official CPI numbers for food.

items.name: Local Bus Ticket (city)
items.fact: City bus fares are often politically controlled — Delhi's DTC bus fares went unchanged for nearly a decade before a 2019 hike, making them one of the cheapest commutes in the world.

items.name: Parle-G Biscuit (small pack)
items.fact: Parle-G famously froze its ₹5 pack price for nearly 25 years by quietly shrinking the biscuit count — the ultimate Indian jugaad against inflation.

items.name: Postcard (inland letter)
items.fact: India Post's inland letter card, once the lifeline of millions, cost just ₹1 in 1990 — its slow price rise reflects how thoroughly it's been replaced by WhatsApp.

items.name: Vada Pav (Mumbai street)
items.fact: Mumbai's beloved vada pav was a ₹1 snack in 1990 — Shiv Sena once tried to cap its price at ₹1 for years, making it a political football alongside a culinary icon.

items.name: Newspaper (daily, Hindi)
items.fact: Hindi dailies like Dainik Bhaskar are among the cheapest in the world — at ₹6, they cost less than one WhatsApp forward, subsidised by massive ad revenue.

items.name: Sugarcane Juice (glass)
items.fact: A glass of fresh sugarcane juice was just ₹2 in 1990 — India is the world's second-largest sugar producer, yet the juice has still gone up 15x in price.

items.name: Atta (wheat flour, 1kg)
items.fact: Atta prices are the most politically sensitive in India — governments have fallen over flour prices, and MSP policy directly shapes what the aam aadmi pays.

items.name: Paan (simple, sweet)
items.fact: The humble paan has become a luxury — what cost ₹1 now costs ₹20, while fancy 'Fire Paan' and 'Gold Paan' variants in cities fetch ₹500+.

items.name: Tata Salt (1kg)
items.fact: Tata Salt's 'Desh ka Namak' campaign made it synonymous with patriotism — at ₹24/kg it's still one of the most price-controlled FMCG products in India.

items.name: Pencil (single HB)
items.fact: India is one of the world's largest pencil manufacturers, with Nataraj and Apsara dominating — a single pencil at ₹7 is still one of the best-value school supplies on earth.

items.name: Nimbu Paani (street lemonade)
items.fact: A glass of nimbu paani from a roadside stall was just ₹1 in 1990 — now at ₹30, it's cheaper than any bottled drink but has risen 30x in three decades.

items.name: Agarbatti (pack of 10)
items.fact: India produces 90% of the world's agarbattis — ironically, a 2020 government ban on Chinese bamboo imports triggered a domestic agarbatti price spike overnight.

items.name: Glucose Biscuit (Britannia, small)
items.fact: Glucose biscuits were the original Indian energy bar — distributed in government schools and anganwadis for decades, they're now facing stiff competition from ₹5 cream biscuit brands.

items.name: Auto Rickshaw (1km, city)
items.fact: Auto fares are set by state governments and revised roughly every 5 years — drivers often refuse metered fares entirely, making the 'official' price largely fictional.

items.name: Hajmola (single tablet)
items.fact: Dabur's Hajmola tablet held at ₹1 for nearly two decades — it's sold over 100 crore tablets a year, making it India's most-consumed digestive product per rupee.

items.name: Maida (refined flour, 500g)
items.fact: Maida powers everything from puris to paratha — India's annual consumption exceeds 10 million tonnes, yet nutritionists have been calling it 'white poison' for three decades.

items.name: Cello Pen (ballpoint, single)
items.fact: Cello is India's largest pen maker, producing over 3 crore pens a day — their flagship ballpoint has barely changed price in 15 years, a quiet victory against stationery inflation.

items.name: Poha (flattened rice, 500g)
items.fact: Poha is the breakfast of Central India — Indore's poha-jalebi combo is so culturally entrenched that a politician once promised 'subsidised poha' in an election manifesto.

items.name: Rubber Eraser (Nataraj)
items.fact: The pink Nataraj eraser has been a school staple since the 1960s — at under ₹10, it remains the most unchanged-in-spirit product in the Indian stationery market.

items.name: Instant Noodles (Maggi, single pack)
items.fact: Maggi was banned for 5 months in 2015 over a lead scare — Nestlé destroyed ₹320 crore worth of noodles, yet sales recovered within a year, proof of Indians' undying Maggi love.

items.name: Coconut (single, medium)
items.fact: Coconut prices have risen 18x since 1990, driven by climate stress on Kerala's plantations and surging global demand for coconut oil — the state tree of Kerala is now a premium commodity.

items.name: Turmeric (haldi, 100g)
items.fact: India grows 75% of the world's turmeric, yet domestic prices spiked 60% in 2024 alone due to a drought in Erode, showing how one district's rain can change every dal's colour.

items.name: Multiplex Movie Ticket
items.fact: A PVR ticket cost ₹50 in 2000 — today it's ₹350 for a standard 2D show, while IMAX seats in metros touch ₹1,500, a 30x rise in 25 years.

items.name: Cricket Ball (leather, practice)
items.fact: A good leather cricket ball cost ₹20 in 1990 — India produces 80% of the world's hand-stitched cricket balls, mostly from Meerut and Jalandhar.

items.name: Paperback Novel (English)
items.fact: A Penguin paperback cost ₹30 in 1990 — Chetan Bhagat broke the ₹95 price ceiling in the 2000s to deliberately reach mass Indian readers, revolutionising publishing economics.

items.name: Chicken Biryani (restaurant, single)
items.fact: Biryani is now India's most-ordered food on Swiggy and Zomato — Hyderabadi biryani restaurants that charged ₹20 in 1990 now charge ₹320, yet always have a queue out the door.

items.name: Basic Cotton T-Shirt
items.fact: India is the world's second-largest textile producer, yet a basic branded cotton tee costs ₹380 today — domestic cotton inflation and GST have pushed fast-fashion prices steadily upward.

items.name: Lifebuoy Soap (125g)
items.fact: Lifebuoy has been sold in India since 1895 and remains the country's most-used soap — it famously ran a 'wash hands' campaign in villages decades before COVID made the concept global.

items.name: Railway Platform Ticket
items.fact: Indian Railways hiked platform ticket prices 10x to ₹50 during COVID to discourage crowding — they've never come back down, a rare pandemic-era price hike that stuck permanently.

items.name: Haldiram's Bhujia (200g pack)
items.fact: Haldiram's started as a small Bikaner sweet shop in 1937 — today it's a ₹10,000 crore empire, and its bhujia has become India's most gifted snack during festival season.

items.name: Dettol Antiseptic (100ml)
items.fact: Dettol has been synonymous with 'antiseptic' in India since the 1950s — its distinctive pine smell is so trusted that counterfeit Dettol has been a recurring scam in rural pharmacies.

items.name: Auto Rickshaw (5km, city)
items.fact: A 5km auto ride cost ₹12 in 1990 — app-based autos now often cost more than the metered fare, while Ola and Uber have effectively privatised India's most democratic transport mode.

items.name: School Geometry Box
items.fact: The metal Camlin geometry box was every Indian student's prized possession in the 1990s — it now faces competition from cheap Chinese imports that look identical but snap within a week.

items.name: Badminton Shuttlecock (single feather)
items.fact: A feather shuttlecock cost ₹8 in 1990 — after P.V. Sindhu's Olympic medals, badminton demand surged 40% in India, pushing premium shuttlecock prices to ₹180 per piece.

items.name: Gold Facial at Parlour
items.fact: A 'gold facial' at a neighbourhood parlour cost ₹20 in 1990 — beauty service inflation has outpaced even gold prices themselves, with premium salon facials now touching ₹5,000 in metros.

items.name: Thali Meal (dhaba, full)
items.fact: A full dhaba thali was ₹10 in 1990 — CRISIL's 'Roti Kapda aur Makaan' index tracks thali costs monthly, and dal-based thalis spiked 25% in 2023 due to pulses inflation.

items.name: Colgate Toothpaste (200g)
items.fact: Colgate holds 55% of India's toothpaste market — it fought off Patanjali's herbal disruption in the 2010s by launching its own herbal range, a masterclass in defensive brand strategy.

items.name: Cotton Saree (simple, mill-made)
items.fact: A simple cotton saree cost ₹80 in 1990 — handloom weavers still earn less than minimum wage, while the same saree sold in a mall with a designer label fetches ₹7,000.

items.name: IPL Match Ticket (general stand)
items.fact: IPL didn't exist before 2008 — prices here reflect equivalent cricket match tickets. IPL general stand tickets at ₹600 are considered cheap, while premium boxes sell for ₹50,000+ per seat.

items.name: Cooking Gas Cylinder (subsidised)
items.fact: LPG cylinder prices rose 22x in 35 years — the ₹200 cylinders of 2010 hit ₹900 by 2025, sparking protests and becoming a key issue in every state election.

items.name: AC Sleeper Bus Ticket (100km)
items.fact: A 100km AC sleeper bus journey was ₹30 in 1990 — private operators like RedBus have transformed inter-city travel, but prices fluctuate wildly with dynamic pricing during festivals.

items.name: Sunscreen SPF 50 (50ml)
items.fact: Sunscreen was a niche product in India until the 2010s — dermatologists' social media campaigns pushed SPF 50 into everyday skincare, creating a ₹3,000 crore sun care market by 2023.

items.name: Cadbury Dairy Milk (large bar, 160g)
items.fact: Cadbury's famous 'Kuch Meetha Ho Jaaye' campaign shifted chocolate from a children's snack to an adult gifting category — and the bar itself shrank 20% in weight as prices doubled.

items.name: Reebok/Nike Basic Flip Flops
items.fact: Branded flip flops at ₹499 compete with Bata's ₹99 Hawai chappals — yet Nike and Reebok sell millions in India by positioning basic rubber soles as 'athleisure' lifestyle products.

items.name: Domino's Pizza (medium, Margherita)
items.fact: Domino's entered India in 1996 — its '30 minutes or free' guarantee was scrapped in 2012 after courts ruled it encouraged rash driving, but it still dominates the ₹20,000 crore pizza market.

items.name: Wool Sweater (machine-made)
items.fact: A basic woollen sweater from Ludhiana cost ₹80 in 1990 — Punjab's knitwear industry supplies 95% of India's domestic sweater market but faces existential threat from cheaper Chinese imports.

items.name: Paracetamol Strip (10 tablets)
items.fact: Branded paracetamol like Crocin cost ₹5 for a strip in 1990 — today at ₹120, pharma companies offset NPPA price controls on generics by launching "branded" variants at a premium.

items.name: Branded Running Shoes (Nike/Adidas entry)
items.fact: Nike entered India in 1995 — entry-level running shoes at ₹5,000 are now considered budget for the brand, while limited-edition Air Jordans resell for ₹50,000+ on StockX India.

items.name: Monthly Gym Membership (mid-range)
items.fact: A gym membership was a ₹100 luxury in 1990 — Cult.fit's ₹2,000/month disrupted traditional gyms in the 2010s, while Anytime Fitness brought 24/7 global gym culture to Indian metros.

items.name: Gold Earrings (22K, 2g)
items.fact: Gold at ₹15,000 for 2g earrings reflects a 19x price rise since 1990 — Indian households hold an estimated 25,000 tonnes of gold, more than the RBI's official reserves.

items.name: Economy Flight Ticket (Delhi–Mumbai)
items.fact: Air Deccan's 2003 launch brought ₹500 'no-frills' fares to India — but budget airlines are often more expensive than trains on last-minute bookings, especially on festival weekends.

items.name: Pressure Cooker (Hawkins, 5L)
items.fact: Hawkins and Prestige have dominated Indian kitchens since the 1970s — the pressure cooker whistle is so iconic that it's been used in film scores and is considered a quintessential sound of Indian home life.

items.name: Cricket Bat (SS/SG, Kashmir willow)
items.fact: Kashmir willow bats were the affordable alternative to expensive English willow — but 30 years of conflict in Kashmir devastated the willow orchards, pushing even basic bat prices sharply upward.

items.name: DSLR Camera Lens Cap (original, 58mm)
items.fact: Camera accessories barely inflated because import duties and competition kept prices in check — but counterfeit Canon and Nikon accessories flooded Indian e-commerce, making buyers wary of cheap listings.

items.name: Washing Machine (semi-auto, 7kg)
items.fact: Semi-automatic washing machines barely inflated for 20 years as competition from Whirlpool, LG and Samsung forced manufacturers to absorb costs — it's one of the rare home appliances that defied inflation.

items.name: Silk Saree (Mysore/Kanchipuram basic)
items.fact: A basic Kanchipuram silk saree costs ₹10,000 today — master weavers who make them often earn less than ₹500/day, while the same sarees are resold at 300% markup in Delhi boutiques.

items.name: Annual School Fees (private, Class 5)
items.fact: Private school fees have risen 25x since 1990 — unregulated fee hikes remain the biggest financial anxiety for India's middle class, and several states have passed but failed to enforce fee-cap laws.

items.name: Royal Enfield Bullet Service (full)
items.fact: Royal Enfield's full service cost ₹300 in 1990 — the 'Bullet' is the world's longest-produced motorcycle model, and its owners form one of India's most loyal and tribal consumer communities.

items.name: LED TV (32-inch, HD)
items.fact: A 32-inch TV cost ₹14,000 in 2010 — Chinese brands like Xiaomi and TCL crashed prices by 40% after 2017, making a full HD 32-inch TV cheaper in 2025 than it was in 2010.

items.name: Yoga Mat (branded, 6mm)
items.fact: Yoga mats cost ₹80 in 1990 and were found only in ashrams — after the UN declared International Yoga Day in 2015, India's yoga product market exploded to ₹2,500 crore within five years.

items.name: Mixer Grinder (Preethi/Sujata, 750W)
items.fact: The mixer grinder replaced the traditional sil-batta grinding stone in Indian kitchens within a single generation — Preethi, founded in Chennai in 1978, now exports to 40 countries.

items.name: Steel Almirah (single door)
items.fact: The steel almirah has been the cornerstone of Indian household storage since the 1960s — passed down through generations, a good almirah outlasts the family's first three TVs combined.

items.name: Branded Sunglasses (Ray-Ban Wayfarer)
items.fact: Ray-Ban Wayfarers were aspirational status symbols in 1990s India at ₹700 — today at ₹8,000, they compete with domestic brands like Fastrack that sell near-identical styles for ₹800.

items.name: Bluetooth Speaker (JBL Go, compact)
items.fact: Portable speakers have actually gotten cheaper due to tech commoditisation — the JBL Go now costs less in real terms than a basic cassette player did in 1990, and sounds exponentially better.

items.name: Medical Insurance (basic, individual, annual)
items.fact: Basic health insurance was just ₹500 in 1990 — post-COVID premiums jumped 30–40% in two years as insurers recalibrated risk, making affordable coverage the biggest financial challenge for India's middle class.

items.name: Monthly Metro Pass (Delhi, all zones)
items.fact: Delhi Metro opened in 2002 — the 2017 fare hike doubled some fares overnight, sparking massive protests, proof that metro pricing is as politically charged as petrol prices in India.

items.name: Tailored Suit (men's, fabric + stitching)
items.fact: A tailored suit cost ₹600 in 1990 — the 'Nehru jacket' remains an Indian power suit alternative, but a proper bespoke suit from a Mumbai or Kolkata tailor now costs 22x what it did in 1990.

items.name: OTT Annual Subscription (Netflix Basic)
items.fact: Netflix launched in India in 2016 at ₹499/month — it later introduced a ₹149 mobile-only plan exclusively for India, the first country where it offered such deep localised pricing.

items.name: Bicycle (Atlas/Hero, adult basic)
items.fact: India was once the world's largest bicycle market — today it's overtaken by e-bikes and motorcycles, and a basic Hero bicycle at ₹7,500 costs more than a cheap two-wheeler EMI in some schemes.

items.name: Trekking Backpack (60L, domestic brand)
items.fact: Trekking was a niche activity in 1990 — India's adventure tourism market crossed ₹1,000 crore by 2023, with Himachal Pradesh and Uttarakhand trails now hosting more Instagram reels than serious mountaineers.

items.name: Inverter Battery (Exide, 150Ah)
items.fact: Invertors became a middle-class necessity in the 1990s power crisis — Exide and Amaron batteries are sold in nearly every Indian town, making India the world's third-largest lead-acid battery market.

items.name: Hair Transplant (per 1000 grafts)
items.fact: Hair transplants have barely risen in price since 2010 as competition among clinics exploded — India now performs over 200,000 procedures annually, becoming one of the world's top medical tourism destinations for hair restoration.

items.name: Smartphone (iPhone 16, base model)
items.fact: The first iPhone launched globally in 2007 — in India, high import duties mean an iPhone costs 20–30% more than in the US, prompting thousands of NRI 'gift' phone requests every year.

items.name: Honda Activa Scooter (base model)
items.fact: The Honda Activa has been India's best-selling vehicle for over a decade — more Activas are sold each year than all cars combined, making it the defining machine of Indian middle-class mobility.

items.name: Mid-range Android Smartphone (Samsung M-series)
items.fact: Mid-range smartphones have barely changed in nominal price since 2015 due to Chinese competition — Xiaomi's 2014 India entry crashed the ₹10,000-₹20,000 segment, giving Indians flagship features at budget prices.

items.name: Gold (24K, 10 grams)
items.fact: Gold at ₹90,000 per 10g represents a 27x rise since 1990 — India consumes 800–900 tonnes of gold annually, second only to China, and smuggling still accounts for an estimated 200 tonnes per year.

items.name: Entry-Level Laptop (HP/Dell, 8GB RAM)
items.fact: A basic laptop cost ₹80,000 in 1990 — the price fell 50% in real terms over 20 years, making laptops accessible to crores of first-generation computer users, transforming India's IT workforce.

items.name: 55-inch 4K Smart TV
items.fact: A 55-inch 4K TV cost over ₹1 lakh in 2010 — Chinese brands like Xiaomi, TCL and Hisense halved prices by 2020, democratising large-screen viewing and accelerating the death of the cable TV box.

items.name: Tata Tiago (entry car, on-road Delhi)
items.fact: The cheapest new Tata car cost ₹1.5 lakh in 1990 — Maruti 800 at ₹47,500 was the dream in 1983, but its successor models inflated 10x, while road taxes and insurance added 20% on top.

items.name: Refrigerator (double door, 340L)
items.fact: A double-door refrigerator cost ₹12,000 in 1990 and barely tripled in 35 years — appliance inflation has been remarkably subdued due to fierce brand competition from LG, Samsung and Godrej.

items.name: Air Conditioner (1.5 Ton, Split, 5-star)
items.fact: ACs were an upper-class luxury in 1990 at ₹25,000 — India's AC market grew 20% in 2023 alone as extreme heat events pushed even lower-middle-class households to buy on EMI for the first time.

items.name: Royal Enfield Classic 350 (base)
items.fact: The Royal Enfield Classic 350 was just ₹25,000 in 1990 — today at ₹2.2 lakh it's become a status symbol that young urban professionals buy not for commuting but for weekend identity.

items.name: Diamond Solitaire Ring (0.25 carat)
items.fact: Surat cuts and polishes 90% of the world's diamonds — yet an Indian buying a diamond ring in India pays import duty markup on diamonds that may have originated in India itself.

items.name: UPSC Coaching (1-year classroom program)
items.fact: UPSC coaching fees have risen 83x since 1990 — Mukherjee Nagar in Delhi is Asia's largest coaching cluster, and families take loans to pay for courses to crack an exam where the selection rate is 0.1%.

items.name: Tata SafariNEXT/MG Hector (mid SUV)
items.fact: Mid-size SUVs have replaced sedans as India's aspirational family car — the segment grew 35% in 2023 as buyers rejected hatchbacks in favour of ground clearance, the ultimate status signal on Indian roads.

items.name: Smart Watch (Apple Watch SE)
items.fact: Smartwatches didn't exist until 2015 — Apple Watch launched in India that year at ₹30,000, while competing Noise and boAt brands now offer smartwatches for ₹1,500, collapsing the category's price floor.

items.name: Solar Panel System (1kW, rooftop)
items.fact: A 1kW solar system cost ₹1.5 lakh in 1990 — prices have fallen 83% in 35 years, and India's PM Surya Ghar Yojana offers ₹78,000 subsidy per household, making rooftop solar finally viable for the middle class.

items.name: Wedding Photography (full day, DSLR)
items.fact: Wedding photography was ₹2,000 in 1990 — drone footage, cinematic reels and 'pre-wedding shoots' have inflated the category 75x, making a South Indian wedding photographer's package pricier than most honeymoon flights.

items.name: Electric Scooter (Ola S1 Pro)
items.fact: Ola Electric's 2021 launch triggered India's EV scooter war — after government FAME subsidies were cut in 2023, prices jumped ₹25,000 overnight, leading to a viral consumer backlash on social media.

items.name: MBA Fee (IIM-A, 2-year program)
items.fact: IIM Ahmedabad's MBA cost ₹20,000 in 1990 — it's now ₹28 lakh, a 1,400x rise, though IIM-A graduates average ₹34 lakh starting salary, making it perhaps India's highest-ROI educational investment.

items.name: Tata Nexon EV (base variant)
items.fact: The Nexon EV launched in 2020 and immediately became India's best-selling electric car — it has a 60% market share in EVs, proof that 'Made in India' can dominate a new technology category from day one.

items.name: OLED TV (55-inch, LG/Sony)
items.fact: OLED TVs cost ₹3.5 lakh in 2010 — the 78% price drop by 2025 mirrors Moore's Law applied to display tech, and India's premium TV segment grew 45% in 2023 as post-COVID 'homebody luxury' spending surged.

items.name: Gaming Console (PlayStation 5)
items.fact: The PS5 launched in India at ₹50,000 — Sony charges one of the world's highest prices in India due to import duties, while Indian gamers increasingly switch to PC gaming to avoid the console premium.

items.name: Mumbai–London Economy Flight
items.fact: A Mumbai–London economy ticket cost ₹25,000 in 1990 — adjusted for inflation, international air travel has actually become cheaper in real terms, democratising what was once an elite luxury.

items.name: Gold Biscuit (100 grams, 24K)
items.fact: 100g of gold cost ₹33,000 in 1990 — it's now ₹9 lakh, a 27x rise that has made gold the single best-performing asset class in India over 35 years, outperforming FDs, real estate and most equity funds.

items.name: Home Loan (₹50L, total interest paid, 20yr)
items.fact: Home loan rates peaked at 18% in the early 1990s — by 2021 they hit historic lows of 6.5%, before RBI hikes pushed them back to 9%, showing how interest rate cycles determine middle-class dreams.

items.name: Luxury Wristwatch (Titan Nebula, gold)
items.fact: Titan's Nebula collection uses solid gold and precious stones — it was India's first 'luxury for Indians' watch brand, launched in 1994 to compete with Swiss imports without the import duty markup.
